California
Fair POlitical
Practices Commission
March ■9, ■99■
John Woodhead
city Attorney
3900 Main Street
Riverside, cA 92522
Re: Your Request for Advice
our File No, A-90-768
Dear Mr. woodhead:
You have requested advice on behalf of City of Riverside
Mayor Terry r,rizzel' concerning application of the conflict-of-
ini"r"=t piovisions of the poiitiiar 
Reform Act (the rra"1rr1.1 The
foll-owing advice is based uPon the facts provided in your letters
and my .r,]rneror= telephone conversations with !Ir- Nornan Herring,
chief Assistant City Attorney. After the last conversation, you
faxed additional information on February 22, L99]-.
This letter concerns ylayor Frizzel's abitity to participate
j-n future decisions of the Riverside city council'. we make no
comrnLnt on ltayor Eri-zzeJ-'s participation in any past decisions.
(Regulation t8239 (c) (4) (A), copy enclosed.) In addition, our
advice is liurited only to provisions of the Act- we cannot
provide advice about other conflict-of-interest 
laws, such as
covernment Code Section 1090.
OUESTIONS
1. Under the Act, nay Mayor Frj.zzel participate in the
Rancho La Sierra specific plan i,f :
(a) the Diaz property is included and the specific plan will
include property within 300 feet of the mayor's home?
(b) the Diaz property is not included and the specific plan
will include property approxirnately 600 feet from the mayor's
horne?
1 Go.r"rr,*"nt code Sections 81ooo-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the Governnent code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission
regulations appear at 2 caLifornia Code of Regulations Section
L8ooo, et seq. AII references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
428 J Street,Suitc 800 ● P.0.Box 807 0 Sacramento CA 95804・0807 0 (916)322´ 5660


File No. A-9 0-7 68
Page 2
2- Under the Act, may I'layor Frizzel participate
general plan revj.sion process (which does not include
proposals regarding the Rancho La Sierra lands) ?
3. May Mayor Frizzel-, a named defendant in the
participate in rnatters concerning pending litigation
Rancho La sierra?
in the
spec i f ic
Iawsuit,
involving
CONCLUSIONS
1(a). 
ylayor Frizzel may not participate in any governmental
decisions concerning the specific plan if the Diaz property is
included, unless the decision will have no financial effect upon
her property. Hosrever, ylayor Fr:-zzel may exercise her veto power
if her participation is legally required.
1(b) . ylayor Erizzel nay not participate in any governmental
decisions concerning the specific plan (which includes Property
within 5oo feet of her horne) if the decision will have a foresee-
able financial effect of $Lo,ooo or more on the fair market value
of her property. However, Ylayor Frizzel nay exercise her veto
power if her participation is legally required.
2. Since the general plan revision does not incfude propos-
a]s regarding the Rancho La Sierra 1ands, lqaYor Frizze] nay
participate in the general plan process if it is not reasonably
ioreseeabl-e that there will be a materj-al financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generall-y, on her
property .
3. The mayor may not participate in her official 
capacity in
any litigration decisions which will have a reasonably foreseeabfe
rnaterial f j.nancial effect on her real property interest. 
Also, as
a named defendant in the lawsuit, she may not participate in any
decisions which will result in a $25o increase or decrease in her
or her family's personal expenses, income, assets or liabil-ities.
FACTS
The city of Riverside is currently in the process of revising
its general p1an. This process will involve numerous public hear-
ings and meetings of the city council, chaired by Mayor FrizzeL.
Under the city charter, the mayor nay only vote on speci.f ic rnat-
ters to break a tie and veto any matter which is then subject to
an override by the members of the city council. 
The nayor also
serves as an ex-officio rnernber of the city council, subcommittee on
land use, which will also consider th_e general plan.
The city has begun the preparation bf a specific plan, which
is a long-range plan for a 700 acre piece of Property known as
Rancho r,i sieira. 
The general p1a4.ievision will defer to the
specific plan in al-L considerations of land use for Rancho La
Sierra and wiII not include specific proposals regarding the
Rancho La Sierra property.


File No. A-9o-768
Page 3
Rancho La sierra is owned by several entities, including a
partnership named the Arlington croup. The Arlington Group has
filed an action in federal district court against the city, the
city council and the mayor. The plaintiffs 
allege that they have
been deprived of their federal constitutional rights as a result
of the city's refusal to process their proposed subdivision.
Pursuant to the suggestion of the federal judge presiding over the
case, the parties have discussed settlement and, in that connec-
tion, the oyrners of Rancho La Sierra have participated in the
general plan amendnent study and have suqgested a specific plan
for the property,
The Diaz property, which is contig'uous to Rancho La sierra,
is directly across the street from Mayor Frizzel's home. The
Arlington Group has acquired development rights on the Diaz
property. This property nay be included in the specific plan for
Rancho La sierra, but that decision has not been made yet. 
The
decision will be rnade in conjunction with the selection of the
consultant .
The general plan revision process, which is expected to be
completed by Septernber 20, lggL, will address all land use deci-
sions in the city except those deferred to a specific p1an. The
specific plan for Rancho La Sierra will certainly include property
approxinatel-y 600 feet from the mayor's hone. However, it is
likeIy that the specific plan wilt also incLude the Diaz property,
which is within 300 feet of the nayor's home.
ANALYS I S
The Act prohibits a public official- from rnaking, participat-
ing in, or usj-ng his official 
position to infl,uence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to knoe/ he has a
financial interest. 
(Section 8710o.) A public official 
has a
financiat interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision wilf have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public Aenerally, on the
officiaf 
or a rnember of his inmediate family or on, among other
things:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investrnent liorth
one thousand dollars ($1000) or more.
(b) Any real property in which the publ,ic
official 
has a direct or indirect i.nterest worth
one thousand doLlars ($1o00) or .more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other.than loans by a conmercial lending
institution in thej regular course of business on
terms available to the public rrithout regard to


Fi■e No. A-90-768
Page 4
official 
status, aggregating two trundred fifty dol-
lars ($250) or more in value provided to, received
by or pronised to the public official 
within 12
months prior to the titne when the decision is rnade.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
section 87103 (a) -(d) .
Mayor FrizzeL's residence is an interest in real property,
defined in Section 82033 as fol-lolrs:
rrlnterest in real propertytr includes any
leasehold, beneficial, or ownership .l,nterest or an
option to acquire such an interest in real property
l-ocated in the jurisdiction 
owned directly,
indirectly or beneficj,ally by the public official,
or other fi1er, or his or her irnroediate farnily if
the fair market value of the interest is one
thousand dolLars ($1,000) or more....
Makinq. Participatinq in Makinq or Attenptino to Influence a
Governmental Decision
Mayor Frizzel makes a governmental decj,sion whenever she
votes on a matter, commits the city to a course of action, or
enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of the city.
(Regulation 18700 (b) . ) In addition, she participates in a
governmental decision when, acting within the authority of her
position, she:
(1) Negotiates, without significant substan-
tive review, with a governmental entity or private
person regarding the decision; or
(2) Advises or nakes recommendations to the
decisi,on-maker, either directly or without
sj-gnif icant intervening substantive review, by:
(A) conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of
j udgrrnent on the part of the official 
or
designated employee and the purpose of which
is to influence the decision; or
(B) Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinj,on, orally or' in writing,
which requires the exercise. of judganent on the
part of the offlcial 
or designated employee


Fi■e No. A-90-768
Page 5
and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision.
Regulation 1870O (c)
In addition, under Regulation 187O0.1(a), Mayor Frizzel uses
her official 
position to influence a decision if:
...for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official 
contacts, or appears before, or otherwise
attempts to influence, any nenber, officer,
employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to
influence include, but are not linited to,
appearances or contacts by the official 
on behalf
of a business entity, client, or customer.
If it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision !,/i11 have a
material financial effect on Mayor Frizzelts home and property
interest, she must disqualify frorn naking or participating in
naking that decision. She also nay not use her official position
to influence the decision.
Foreseeabi l itv
The effect of a decision j-s reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it wil-l occur. certainty is not
required; however, an effect that is merely a possibility is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Do$rnev cares v. Downev communitv
Development Com. (1987) 196 Ca1.App.3d 983; In re Thorner (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 198, copy enclosed.)
Because of the proxinity of the mayor's home to the Rancho La
Sierra property, it is reasonably foreseeable that the specific
pl-an may have a financial effect on her real property interest.
Therefore, it is necessary to determine lrhether the effect on her
property will be naterial.
Mater ial itv
The commj,ssion has adopted several, regulations which define
rnaterial financial effect. 
Regulation 18702 sets forth the
general guidelines for deterrnining whether an official's 
financial
interest in a decision is "naterial" as required by Section 87103.
If the officiaL's financial interest is directlv involved in the
decision, ReguJ.ation lA702.1 applies to deterrnine rnateriality. 
If
the official's 
financial interest is indirectlv affected by the
decision, Regulations LA702.2 through. 78702.5 apply to deterrnine
whether the effect of the decision is rnaterial.
The Specific Plan
Regnrlation 7a702.3 (copy- enctosea; contains guidelines for
deterrnining when the effect of a decision is material as to real


Fi■e No. A-90-768
Page 6
property which is indirectly involved in the decision. Regulation
1a7o2.3 states in pertinent part:
(a) The effect of a decision is naterial as
to real property in which an official 
has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not
including a leasehold interest), if any of the foL-
lowing applies:
(1) The real proPerty in which the of-
ficial 
has an interest, or any part of that
real property, is l-ocated within a 300 foot
radius of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the
official's 
real property interest.
(2) The decision involves construction
of, or irnproveruents to, streets, water, sewer,
storm drainage or sinil-ar facilities, 
and the
reaL property in wtrich the official 
has an
interest will recej-ve new or substantially
irnproved services.
(3) The real proPerty in which the of-
ficial 
has an interest is located outside a
radius of 3oo feet and any part of the real
property is located within a radius of 2,5oo
feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the decision wil-I
have a reasonably foreseeable financiaL effect
of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000)
or nore on the fair market value of the
real property in which the official 
has
an interest r' or
(B) WiIl affect the rental val-ue of
the property bY $1,ooo or more Per 12
month period.
The nayor's residence is located within 300 feet frorn the
Diaz property, which tnay be included in the specific.plan. Pursu-
ant to Regulation La7o2.3 (a) (1), the effect of a decision on her
property is deened naterial and she may not participate in a
governrnental decision, unless the decisibn will have no financial-
effect upon her property interest.


File No. A-9O-768
Page 7
Her residence is al.so located approximately 600 feet from the
Rancho La Sierra property, which is definitely included in the
specific plan. ff it is reasonably foreseeable that the Rancho La
sierra specific plan will have an effect of $1o,ooo or more on the
fair narket value of her hone, she may not participate in any
governmental decisions concerning the specific plan.
Under Regmlation 18702.3(d), there are a number of factors
srhich nust be considered in deternining whether the decision will
have a financial effect of $10,00o or more. These factors
include, but are not limited to:
(1) The proxiroity of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the nagnitude of
the proposed project or change in use in relation-
ship to the property in which the official 
has an
interest,'
(2) I,Ihether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision wiIl affect the development potential
or income producing potential of the proPerty;
(3) In addition to the foregoing, in the case
of residential property, r.rhether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will resuft in a
change to the character of the neighborhood includ-
ing, but not limited to, effects on traffic, 
view,
privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air enis-
sions, or sinrilar traits of the neighborhood.
It is incurnbent upon ylaYot Frizzel to consider the above fac-
tors and to make a good faith estirnate of the financiaL effect-
If the specific pfan for Rancho La Sierra wil} have an effect of
$l-orooo or more on the fair market value of her home, she may not
participate in her official 
capacity in the decisions concerning
the specif ic pl-an.
Pursuant to ReguLati on 78702.3 (a) (2) the effect of a decj'sion
is also deemed materiaL if the decision j-nvoLves construction of,
or improvenents to, streets, vrater, sewer, storm drainage or
sirnilar facilities, 
and the mayor's property will receive any of
these ne!, or substantially irnproved services. we do not have any
facts regarding the specific plan and whether any of these
improvements are included. Therefore, these factors nust also be
taken into consideration by Mayor FrizzeJ-.
The General PIan and Public GenerallY
The general plan is a comprehensive planning docunent which
affects the entire city of Riverside., The general plan revision
will defer to the specifid plan in a'11 consideratj.ons of land use
for Rancho La Sierra and will- not include specific proposals
regarding the Rancho La Sierra lands.


Fil-e No. A-9o-768
Page I
Mayor Frizzel, may participate in the qeneral' plan process as
long as the decisions will not have a materiaL financial- effect on
her or on her property. In addition, under section 87103,
disqualification in a decision is not required if tle effect of
the decision on the officiaf's 
interest is not distinguishable
from the effect on the general public; the decision must affect
the offici,al's 
interest in substantially the same rnanner as j't
$rould affect a significant segment of the public.
since all residents of the juri,sdiction are constituents of
the official, 
the residents in the city's jurisdiction 
cornprise
the rpublic.tr 
(In re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1; In r? glren
Gg7 61 2 r'PPc op- z7 copies enclosed.) Thus, disgualification is
required unless the general plan decisions wiII affect Mayor
Frizzel's property in substantially the same manner as it will
affect all- reaidents of the city, or a significant segTment of the
residents of the city.
Li-tiqation Decisions
The ArLington group, the plaintiff 
in the litigation 
against
the city and tie n"for,-iras development rights to property within
:00 feel of Mayor Fiizzel's home. The mayor's potential
participation -in the litigation 
coul-d include decisions concerning
settlemint of legat claims and strategic choices involving the
city and individually naned defendants. The mayor also conducts
cloied sessions with Iega1 counsel regarding pending J- j-tigation'
The mayor may not participate in any litigation 
decisions
which will have a reasonably foreseeable naterial financial effect
on her property, as outlined above in Regulation 18702.3. Ad-
ditionally; 
since the mayor is also a named defendant in the
Iawsui.t, ste nay not participate in any decisions which will
result in either her or her irnrnediate family's personal expenses,
incone, assets (other than interests in real property), or Ii-
abilitj-es increasing or decreasing by 9250. (Regulation
L87 02 . r- (a) (4).)
Leqall,v Required ParticiPation
Section 87L01 pernits an otherwise disqualified official to
make or participate in the making of a governmental decision when
his or hlr partlcipation is tegal.ly required. Regulation 18701
states that I'a public official 
is not 1egally required to.make or
to participate -in the making of a governmental decision within the
r..-r'ri.rg of Government code Section 8?101 untess there exists no
alternitive 
source of decision consistent trith the purposes and
terns of the statute authorizing the dec-ision.I'
224 Car. Rptr. 557 , ,th,e
that the mayor did not
In Af f orllable Heus:Ln
cal-. App.3d 484 ,
18701 and ruled
court reli.ed
violate the
upon Regulation
Act when she


FiIe No. A-90-7 68
Page 9
vetoed a rent control ordinance, even though she had a financiat
interest in the decision. The court reasoned that the nayor,s
participation by exercising the veto $ras legally reguired based
upon the language in the city charter.
You have stated that under the city charter for the city of
Riverside, the mayor may vote on specific matters to break a tie
and veto any matter $rhich is then subject to an override by the
nenbers of the city council. Regarding llayor Frizzel's veto power
in decisions where she has a financj,al interest, we suggest that
you review the reasoning of the above-referenced case in relation
to the language in your city charter.
However, please note that the above discussion regarding the
specif ic plan ref ers to !.{ayor Frizzel's trparticipationtr , which
under the Act is nuch broader than just the ability to veto, 
The
Affordable Housinq Altiance v. Feinstein case is lirnited
specifically and only to the mayor's veto power under the city
charter. In this case the court also notes, citing section 87101,
that the fact that an official's 
vote is needed to break a tie
does not make his or her participation 1ega1ly required for
purposes of that section.
Particir:atinq in a Non-official Capacitv
Both Requl,ation 18700(d) (2) and Regulatj,on 18700.1(b) (1)
(copies encl-osed) allor., a public official 
to appear, as any nember
of the general public, before an agency in the course of its
prescribed governmental function, to represent hj.mself or herself
on matters related solely to his or her personal interests. 
A
rrpersonal interest't includes an j-nterest in real property which is
whotl-y owned by the official, or members of his or her immediate
fanily. 
(Regulation 18700. r- (b) (1) (A) . )
Regul-ation 187o0.1(b) (2) further a11ows a disqualified public
official 
to communicate with the general public or the press.
Thus, the Act allows a public official 
to comment, as a member of
the public, at public neetings, forums, hearings or to the press
on issues related so1e1y to the effect of a decision on her
property. once the official 
has formally disqualified herself by
announcing her disgual ification and the reason therefor on the
public record (Regulation 18700(b) (5)), her cornments should be
made under the same procedures applicable to comments from members
of the pub1,ic. For example, she is required to address the city
council from the audience, rather than from her mayor's seat.
Regulations 18700 and 18700.1 permit her to testify concerning the
effects of the proposed project on her property, but she may not
testify on behalf of any other person or,. group.


FiIe No. A-9O-768
Page 10
I trust this anstIers your questions. If you have any further
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(9L6) 322-5901.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ceneral counsel

or designated employee

and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision.
Regulation 1870O (c)
In addition, under Regulation 187O0.1(a), Mayor Frizzel uses
her official 
position to influence a decision if:
...for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official 
contacts, or appears before, or otherwise
attempts to influence, any nenber, officer,

on behalf

If it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision !,/i11 have a
material financial effect on Mayor Frizzelts home and property
interest, she must disqualify frorn naking or participating in
naking that decision. She also nay not use her official position
to influence the decision.
Foreseeabi l itv
The effect of a decision j-s reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it wil-l occur. certainty is not
required; however, an effect that is merely a possibility is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Do$rnev cares v. Downev communitv
Development Com. (1987) 196 Ca1.App.3d 983; In re Thorner (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 198, copy enclosed.)
Because of the proxinity of the mayor's home to the Rancho La
Sierra property, it is reasonably foreseeable that the specific
pl-an may have a financial effect on her real property interest.
Therefore, it is necessary to determine lrhether the effect on her
property will be naterial.
Mater ial itv
The commj,ssion has adopted several, regulations which define
rnaterial financial effect. 
Regulation 18702 sets forth the
general guidelines for deterrnining whether an official's 
financial
interest in a decision is "naterial" as required by Section 87103.
If the officiaL's financial interest is directlv involved in the
decision, ReguJ.ation lA702.1 applies to deterrnine rnateriality. 
If
the official's 
financial interest is indirectlv affected by the
decision, Regulations LA702.2 through. 78702.5 apply to deterrnine
whether the effect of the decision is rnaterial.
The Specific Plan
Regnrlation 7a702.3 (copy- enctosea; contains guidelines for
deterrnining when the effect of a decision is material as to real


Fi■e No. A-90-768
property which is indirectly involved in the decision. Regulation
1a7o2.3 states in pertinent part:
(a) The effect of a decision is naterial as
to real property in which an official 
has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not
including a leasehold interest), if any of the foL-
lowing applies:
(1) The real proPerty in which the of-
ficial 
has an interest, or any part of that
real property, is l-ocated within a 300 foot
radius of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the
official's 
real property interest.
(2) The decision involves construction
of, or irnproveruents to, streets, water, sewer,
storm drainage or sinil-ar facilities, 
and the
reaL property in wtrich the official 
has an
interest will recej-ve new or substantially
irnproved services.
(3) The real proPerty in which the of-
ficial 
has an interest is located outside a
radius of 3oo feet and any part of the real
property is located within a radius of 2,5oo
feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the decision wil-I
have a reasonably foreseeable financiaL effect
of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000)
or nore on the fair market value of the
real property in which the official 
has
an interest r' or
(B) WiIl affect the rental val-ue of
the property bY $1,ooo or more Per 12
month period.
The nayor's residence is located within 300 feet frorn the
Diaz property, which tnay be included in the specific.plan. Pursu-
ant to Regulation La7o2.3 (a) (1), the effect of a decision on her
property is deened naterial and she may not participate in a
governrnental decision, unless the decisibn will have no financial-
effect upon her property interest.


File No. A-9O-768
Her residence is al.so located approximately 600 feet from the
Rancho La Sierra property, which is definitely included in the
specific plan. ff it is reasonably foreseeable that the Rancho La
sierra specific plan will have an effect of $1o,ooo or more on the
fair narket value of her hone, she may not participate in any
governmental decisions concerning the specific plan.
Under Regmlation 18702.3(d), there are a number of factors
srhich nust be considered in deternining whether the decision will
have a financial effect of $10,00o or more. These factors
include, but are not limited to:
(1) The proxiroity of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the nagnitude of
the proposed project or change in use in relation-
ship to the property in which the official 
has an
interest,'
(2) I,Ihether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision wiIl affect the development potential
or income producing potential of the proPerty;
(3) In addition to the foregoing, in the case
of residential property, r.rhether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will resuft in a
change to the character of the neighborhood includ-
ing, but not limited to, effects on traffic, 
view,
privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air enis-
sions, or sinrilar traits of the neighborhood.
It is incurnbent upon ylaYot Frizzel to consider the above fac-
tors and to make a good faith estirnate of the financiaL effect-
If the specific pfan for Rancho La Sierra wil} have an effect of
$l-orooo or more on the fair market value of her home, she may not
participate in her official 
capacity in the decisions concerning
the specif ic pl-an.
Pursuant to ReguLati on 78702.3 (a) (2) the effect of a decj'sion
is also deemed materiaL if the decision j-nvoLves construction of,
or improvenents to, streets, vrater, sewer, storm drainage or
sirnilar facilities, 
and the mayor's property will receive any of
these ne!, or substantially irnproved services. we do not have any
facts regarding the specific plan and whether any of these
improvements are included. Therefore, these factors nust also be
taken into consideration by Mayor FrizzeJ-.
The General PIan and Public GenerallY
The general plan is a comprehensive planning docunent which
affects the entire city of Riverside., The general plan revision
will defer to the specifid plan in a'11 consideratj.ons of land use
for Rancho La Sierra and will- not include specific proposals
regarding the Rancho La Sierra lands.


Fil-e No. A-9o-768
Page I
Mayor Frizzel, may participate in the qeneral' plan process as
long as the decisions will not have a materiaL financial- effect on
her or on her property. In addition, under section 87103,
disqualification in a decision is not required if tle effect of
the decision on the officiaf's 
interest is not distinguishable
from the effect on the general public; the decision must affect
the offici,al's 
interest in substantially the same rnanner as j't
$rould affect a significant segment of the public.
since all residents of the juri,sdiction are constituents of
the official, 
the residents in the city's jurisdiction 
cornprise
the rpublic.tr 
(In re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1; In r? glren
Gg7 61 2 r'PPc op- z7 copies enclosed.) Thus, disgualification is
required unless the general plan decisions wiII affect Mayor
Frizzel's property in substantially the same manner as it will
affect all- reaidents of the city, or a significant segTment of the
residents of the city.
Li-tiqation Decisions
The ArLington group, the plaintiff 
in the litigation 
against
the city and tie n"for,-iras development rights to property within
:00 feel of Mayor Fiizzel's home. The mayor's potential
participation -in the litigation 
coul-d include decisions concerning
settlemint of legat claims and strategic choices involving the

The mayor may not participate in any litigation 
decisions
which will have a reasonably foreseeable naterial financial effect
on her property, as outlined above in Regulation 18702.3. Ad-
ditionally; 
since the mayor is also a named defendant in the
Iawsui.t, ste nay not participate in any decisions which will
result in either her or her irnrnediate family's personal expenses,
incone, assets (other than interests in real property), or Ii-
abilitj-es increasing or decreasing by 9250. (Regulation
L87 02 . r- (a) (4).)
Leqall,v Required ParticiPation
Section 87L01 pernits an otherwise disqualified official to
make or participate in the making of a governmental decision when
his or hlr partlcipation is tegal.ly required. Regulation 18701
states that I'a public official 
is not 1egally required to.make or
to participate -in the making of a governmental decision within the
r..-r'ri.rg of Government code Section 8?101 untess there exists no
alternitive 
source of decision consistent trith the purposes and
terns of the statute authorizing the dec-ision.I'
224 Car. Rptr. 557 , ,th,e
that the mayor did not
In Af f orllable Heus:Ln
cal-. App.3d 484 ,
18701 and ruled
court reli.ed
violate the
upon Regulation
Act when she


FiIe No. A-90-7 68
vetoed a rent control ordinance, even though she had a financiat
interest in the decision. The court reasoned that the nayor,s
participation by exercising the veto $ras legally reguired based
upon the language in the city charter.
You have stated that under the city charter for the city of
Riverside, the mayor may vote on specific matters to break a tie
and veto any matter $rhich is then subject to an override by the
nenbers of the city council. Regarding llayor Frizzel's veto power
in decisions where she has a financj,al interest, we suggest that
you review the reasoning of the above-referenced case in relation
to the language in your city charter.
However, please note that the above discussion regarding the
specif ic plan ref ers to !.{ayor Frizzel's trparticipationtr , which
under the Act is nuch broader than just the ability to veto, 
The
Affordable Housinq Altiance v. Feinstein case is lirnited
specifically and only to the mayor's veto power under the city
charter. In this case the court also notes, citing section 87101,
that the fact that an official's 
vote is needed to break a tie
does not make his or her participation 1ega1ly required for
purposes of that section.
Particir:atinq in a Non-official Capacitv
Both Requl,ation 18700(d) (2) and Regulatj,on 18700.1(b) (1)
(copies encl-osed) allor., a public official 
to appear, as any nember
of the general public, before an agency in the course of its
prescribed governmental function, to represent hj.mself or herself
on matters related solely to his or her personal interests. 
A
rrpersonal interest't includes an j-nterest in real property which is
whotl-y owned by the official, or members of his or her immediate
fanily. 
(Regulation 18700. r- (b) (1) (A) . )
Regul-ation 187o0.1(b) (2) further a11ows a disqualified public
official 
to communicate with the general public or the press.
Thus, the Act allows a public official 
to comment, as a member of
the public, at public neetings, forums, hearings or to the press
on issues related so1e1y to the effect of a decision on her
property. once the official 
has formally disqualified herself by
announcing her disgual ification and the reason therefor on the
public record (Regulation 18700(b) (5)), her cornments should be
made under the same procedures applicable to comments from members
of the pub1,ic. For example, she is required to address the city
council from the audience, rather than from her mayor's seat.
Regulations 18700 and 18700.1 permit her to testify concerning the
effects of the proposed project on her property, but she may not
testify on behalf of any other person or,. group.
